chapters.title: Narcissists, life's puppeteers
chapters.text:
We've all seen a children's play or TV show featuring puppets. Aren't they fun? However, have you ever wondered if some people manipulate others in the same way? The truth is that, yes, they do exist and they live among us. They are nothing more and nothing less than narcissists. This summary will help you understand how to identify and interact with narcissists.

Understanding narcissism allows us to identify it in others and analyze our own vulnerability.

The mind of a narcissist is like an iceberg, deep and enigmatic. When dissected, a variety of personality traits are revealed, from self-destruction to manipulation. Particularly in this day and age, the capacity to identify a narcissist, comprehend their fears and strategies, and eventually destroy their control mechanisms is an essential skill.

Are you prepared to discover how to break these puppeteers' strings? Without further ado, let's get to it.

chapters.title: Narcissus: The ultimate narcissist
chapters.text:
The story of young Narcissus can be found in an extract from the third volume of the Roman poet Ovid's Metamorphoses. His image has been used as a symbol of unbridled narcissism since ancient Greece. Raised by the nymph Liriope, he once asked Tiresias, the soothsayer, if he would grow old, and the soothsayer replied, “If you do not get to know yourself, then yes.”

Narcissus’ rejection of people who admired and wanted him and his exceptional beauty defined his life. The nymph Echo, crushed by the disdain, vanished among them until her voice was all that was left.

Those who were disowned begged the gods to punish him and force him to fall in love with someone beyond his reach. The goddess of justice and fairness, Themis, chose to make him fall in love with his own reflection as a punishment for his lack of empathy. Narcissus was consumed by his own conceit and ultimately died as a result of it. He passed away in front of his reflection at the pond's edge. There was only a thin, saffron-colored flower where his body should have been.

A narcissistic personality leads the sufferer to a dramatic end, dragging those who accompany it in that direction. ~ Júlia Pascual

This myth encapsulates the essence of pathological narcissism, classified as a personality disorder. Theodore Millon, an American psychologist, spent a significant portion of his career researching narcissism. Some of these personality types — dependent, histrionic, narcissistic, antisocial, compulsive, avoidant, schizoid, negativistic, schizotypal, borderline, and paranoid — are featured prominently in his theory of personality.

Although identifying a narcissist might seem impossible at first, it can be done. We can recognize narcissists because of certain traits that have been the subject of decades of research. They include:

• They are seductive: They use their confidence to their advantage to captivate and impose their presence.
• They know how to communicate well: One of their defining characteristics is their ability to use language and tone of voice effectively to accomplish their goals.
• They are experts in a specific subject: They use their expertise in a particular field as a means of persuasion.
• They have good social relationships: They often operate in groups and manipulate others to their advantage to appear confident in front of others.

Narcissists lack empathy, making a healthy relationship impossible.

chapters.title: A narcissist in the making
chapters.text:
You might be wondering if narcissists are created or born. Well, it depends on the specific circumstances. Numerous factors, including social and psychological experiences as well as genetic factors, can influence narcissistic personality disorder. The development of the most defining character traits occurs during childhood, making it a critical time in shaping these tendencies.

Three distinct experiences are known to have the potential to trigger the emergence of pathological narcissism during this critical period. Every circumstance results in harm that affects the person's behavior and sense of self.

First of all, we have affectively deprived children, characterized by their parents' emotional absence. To shield themselves from suffering, they grow an armor of haughtiness and egocentrism beyond what is typical for their age. People who experience conditional love are also included in this group, fostering a never-ending approval-seeking culture.

However, overprotected children, brought up in a setting where parents seek to minimize all obstacles for them, grow up feeling entitled to everything and intolerant of frustration. Instead of boosting their confidence, this overprotection undermines their capacity to face challenges, accept rejection, and develop self-worth.

The last group is the mixed group, which includes children with affective deficiencies and absent parents, but also sporadic episodes of overprotection. This combination contributes to a weak and reliant self-image, causing insecurity and fear of abandonment.

Offer your children unconditional love, yet remember to encourage their autonomy and resilience.

Fostering a stable attachment and emotional security is critical if you are a parent. Ensure to set healthy limits, avoid excessive praise and privileges, and lead by example when acknowledging mistakes.

Teaching kids how to overcome challenges and appreciate differences is important and helps create a culture of respect and responsibility. A good mantra is: “To be guides rather than admirers.” Encourage them to develop a sense of humility and respect for themselves and others.

chapters.title: Praise as a defense
chapters.text:
Fortunately, it is easy to distinguish between types of narcissists. But what can you do with people who constantly seek approval from others? Surprisingly, the answer is to applaud them for their actions. It may be hard to believe, but narcissists are approval junkies, at the end of the day. If you want to stop them hurting you, be sure to get on their good side.

By praising them sincerely or slyly, you can get them to let their guard down and win their favor. It's like giving them what they want most: feeling special by getting what they need. Before you can use this strategy, you need to know what kind of narcissist they are.

One can defend oneself against attacks, but in the face of praise one is defenseless. ~ Sigmund Freud

Think about the armor of protection that every narcissist wears. It is often the trait they are most proud of, yet it is their weakness. So, observe how they behave. We can categorize them into two primary groups: overt narcissists, who exhibit grandiosity and are readily identifiable, and covert narcissists, who operate under a facade of shyness or modesty but are, in fact, adept at manipulating and deceiving.

Likewise, we can classify narcissists according to their predominant personality pattern, such as:

• Histrionics: They seek as much attention as possible, surrounding themselves with an audience that can give them the admiration they seek.
• Obsessive compulsive: They control everything around them. They are very perfectionistic and enjoy the fact that they always have control.
• Borderline: They are afraid of abandonment, and to avoid it, they create narratives that make them seem like interesting people by distorting reality. They expose their vulnerability by questioning it.
• Paranoid: They distrust everyone and act extremely defensively.
• Psychopathic: They can become the most dangerous as they seek their own pleasure, regardless of whether or not they hurt others.

Transforming a narcissist’s weakness into your advantage allows you to deal with their manipulation.

chapters.title: Learn to spot manipulation
chapters.text:
Silent or not, we are always communicating, and communication is the most powerful method of influencing others.

There are several types of influence in communication:

• Persuasion: It happens through the use of respectful and strategic communication. The objective is to encourage a person to realize that their goals are similar to those of the narcissist.
• Convincing: The speaker uses this method when they want someone else to align with their ideals and views.
• Manipulation: The manipulator wants to convince someone to pursue an objective that will only favor them. The outcome for the person carrying it out, whether beneficial or harmful, is secondary.

Narcissists often employ these forms of communication to exert control and manipulate others. However, they have many more tricks up their sleeves, such as:

• Love bombing: This tactic is used in romantic relationships to drown a person with compliments, gifts, and appreciation.
• Guilt projection: Narcissists will project their failures and wrongdoings onto others to avoid questioning.
• Victimization: Narcissists often avoid taking responsibility and instead seek sympathy from others to portray themselves as victims.
• Intermittent reinforcement: They accomplish this by rewarding their victims for exhibiting their desired behavior and reprimanding them when they fail.
• The silent treatment: The narcissist becomes distant and indifferent toward the person, as a way of punishing them.
• Conditioned flattery: They use compliments or praise strategically to influence someone's behavior or decisions.
• Narcissistic rage: They might intimidate and threaten their victims when they directly oppose the narcissists.
• Pressure or insistence: This strategy is about criticizing others to reinforce the narcissist's self-esteem.
• Smokescreen: The narcissist might make the victim believe they are going crazy, are mistaken, or exaggerating a situation.
• Smear campaign: The narcissist cooperates with their allies to discredit their victims.
• Emotional blackmail: They deceive victims and instill in them the belief that they are incapable of achieving anything.
• Lies: They omit details to make their version of a story more beneficial to them.
• Taking advantage of family relationships: Narcissists will exploit family dysfunction to validate their toxic behaviors.
• Triangulations: This involves manipulating someone with the help of a trusted ally who is also being manipulated.
• Gaslighting: A common strategy is making the victim believe things that did not actually happen by questioning their actions.

Manipulation is often quiet and unnoticed. Staying alert is vital to escape a narcissist’s clutches.

Did you know? A study published in the Psychology of Popular Media Culture journal found that social media use, especially platforms such as Instagram and Facebook, is associated with an increase in narcissistic traits among users.

chapters.title: Breaking the cycle of abuse
chapters.text:
The dynamics between a narcissist and their victim can often involve a complex interplay. Victims may be caught in this cycle without realizing it, and recognizing the situation and finding a way out can be incredibly challenging. This lack of awareness makes the cycle particularly insidious and hard to escape.

This cycle usually consists of the following phases:

• The first is love bombing when the narcissist sets out to glorify and praise their victim. They recognize the person’s qualities and convince them that they are the center of their universe.
• Then, they quickly enter the contempt phase. This stage is when they will openly demonstrate that nothing their target does is good enough for them and criticize every aspect of their being to discredit their accomplishments.
• After this, they transition into intermittent reinforcement, moving constantly between the previous two stages, seeking to leave the person confused and awaiting their opinion and approval.
• Next, they begin to exercise perverse triangulation. They will employ their contacts, who are just as manipulated as their other victims, to turn them against the victim.
• In the phase of discarding, we reach the point where the narcissist reveals their true intentions and will achieve their goal without stopping to think about the other person’s feelings.

A part of the process that doesn't always happen is called hoovering. If the victim manages to get rid of the narcissist, nothing eliminates the possibility that they will return a couple of years later under the guise that they have changed. Once they feel confident again, they will start the cycle from scratch.

Now that you understand the phases, there are several ways to break away. Let's go through them.

Let’s start with the Gray Rock Method. The victim takes a stoic and aloof attitude to pressure the narcissist to try other strategies, which makes them lose interest over time. Additionally, strategic dialogue is useful. This makes the narcissist think the person understands their way of seeing the world without compromising their character. Instead of confronting them, they may ask questions.

If none of these strategies work, learning to say “no” gracefully, firmly, and often is key.

Keep your expectations realistic; narcissists may not be empathetic or supportive in the ways you hope.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Interacting with someone who has narcissistic personality disorder can be challenging, especially since they often thrive in creating stressful situations. Whether it’s at home or at work, encountering this behavior can affect everyone involved. To navigate such relationships effectively, strategic thinking is key. By learning how to set clear boundaries, you can gradually reduce their impact on your life.

Now that you know how to deal with narcissistic personality disorder and how it works, you can help others get out of toxic relationships. If possible, you can even help a narcissist obtain the professional help they need. Therapy can be very important for both a victims' recovery and for the narcissists themselves to recognize their self-destructive behaviors.

Try this


• Stand in front of a mirror and practice saying your boundaries assertively.
• Choose a positive affirmation or mantra to remind yourself of your worth during tough moments.
• If you're in a work or romantic relationship with a potential narcissist, surround yourself with trusted family and friends.

Read it till the end?
